title: Arabinose dehydrogenase (AraDH) reporter enzyme assay for Haloferax volcanii
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"51y33","text":"In H. volcanii the arabinose dehydrogenase is routinely used as a reporter enzyme. The AraDH (XacB) enzyme catalyzes the oxidation of L-arabinose to L-arabino-1,4-lactone while reducing the cofactor NAD+ or NADP+ [1]. Reduction of the preferred cofactor NADP+ to NADPH or NAD+ to NADH can be measured and quantified via increase of the extinction at 340 nm.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":3,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"x2n25","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0g7kb","text":"To enable the parallel handling of multiple samples the assay was adapted to the microtiter plate format. The “procedure” (see below) describes an application with the araDH (xacB) gene in a translational fusion with the gene of interest.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":168,"length":6},{"style":"italic","offset":175,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"iktfd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"u51g2","text":"To analyze differential gene expression, reporter genes are used as valuable tools [2]. The enzymes encoded by reporter genes should be easy to quantify with cheap, sensitive and robust enzymatic assays. Typically  these reporter proteins fold with high efficiency in different species and are relatively small, monomeric enzymes.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fklv3","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b55pt","text":"To study transcript stabilities or promoter activities, transcriptional fusions can be used. Translational fusions enable analysis of protein stabilities as well as differential translational efficiencies.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0f6yk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ejvyo","text":"For the osmotic adaption to high salt environments, halophilic archaea use a mechanism that is called “salt in” strategy. This results in a cytoplasmic salt concentration as high as in the environment. For this reason, commonly used reporter gene systems for bacteria or eukaryotes can not be used in haloarchaea, because the proteins do not fold and function in the high-salt cytoplasm. However, there are some haloarchaea-specific reporters that have been established, e.g. a haloarchaeal beta-galactosidase [3] and a halotolerant version of the green fluorescent protein (GFP) [4]. Here, we describe the application of the araDH (xacB) gene encoding arabinose dehydrogenase as reporter. It catalyzes the specific oxidation of L-arabinose under preference of the cofactor NADP+ over NAD+ [1].","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":626,"length":6},{"style":"italic","offset":633,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zawng","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"d4pmy","text":"Reagents","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9to57","text":"·         Basal salts: 2.1 M NaCl, 220 mM MgCl2 x 6 H2O, 41 mM MgSO4 x 7 H2O, 13 mM KCl, 9 mM CaCl2 x 2 H2O, 50 mM Tris/HCl pH 7.2","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":46,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":53,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":67,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":74,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":98,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":105,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"w02sx","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"na9qe","text":"·         Tris-KCL-buffer: 0.2 M Tris, 3 M KCl, pH 8.5","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"n5l6e","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"oxiuv","text":"·         Arabinose stock solution: 240 mM L-Arabinose","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6xtry","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4txzb","text":"·         NADP+-solution: 48 mM NADP+ (prepare freshly)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"byikm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"pdrwy","text":"·         AraDH reaction mix: 150 µl Tris-KCl-buffer (final concentration: 0.1 M; 1.5 M), 25 µl arabinose solution (20 mM), 25 µl NADP+-solution (4 mM) + 100 µl lysate","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hozcw","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ylynh","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"38ela","text":"Equipment","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jybob","text":"·         Microtiter plate (MTP) photometer","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"sjoyk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lrecl","text":"·         96 well microtiter plates","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rioph","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2scmc","text":"·         PierceTM BCA Assay Kit (Thermo Scientific)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":16,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4456j","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: 30 ml of complex medium for H. volcanii are inoculated with a plasmid-containing strain in at least three biological replicates. Additionally a culture with an empty vector is grown as a negative control.
After reaching stationary phase, these cultures are used to inoculate a culture of 30 ml fresh medium and cultivate the cells to mid-exponential phase (about 4*10 8 cells/ml).

Then the mid-exponential cells are used to inoculate a culture of 30 ml fresh medium that is cultivated again. The resulting mid-exponential cultures are harvested and used for the assay and Northern blot analysis.

For Northern blot analysis, at least two 2 ml aliquots of mid-exponential cultures are removed and centrifuged (13.000 rpm, 2 minutes, room temperature). The supernatant is discarded, and the pellets can be stored at -80°C and used later for RNA analysis as described in the method “Northern blot analysis” ( https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.2.11264/v1 ).

For the AraDH enzymatic assay, 20 ml of the cell cultures are removed and samples are centrifuged (4000 rpm, 15 minutes, 4°C). The supernatant is discarded and the resulting pellets are resuspended in 5 ml basal salt.
The cells are collected by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 15 minutes, 4°C) again, the supernatant is discarded and the resulting pellets are suspended in 1 ml basal salt.

To break open the harvested cells, sonication is used. The cell suspensions are kept on ice during the process and sonication is performed three times for each 30 seconds (duty cycle 50%, output control 3) and one-minute cooling period.

After lysis, the samples are centrifuged (13.000 rpm, 30 minutes, 4°C) to remove the cell debris, and the supernatant is transferred to a new tube. The resulting cytoplasmic extract is used for the enzyme essay.

The AraDH enzymatic assay is performed in a 96 well plate. First, the MTP photometer is warmed up to 37°C. After that, different cytoplasmic extract dilutions (1:5, 1:10) are made and measured each in two technical replicates.

For each sample, a reaction mix was made, that contained 100 µl cytoplasmic extract dilution, 150 µl 3 M KCl (preheated to 40°C) and 25 µl arabinose solution. The reaction is started by adding 25 µl NADP+-solution.
After starting the reaction, the optical density at 340 nm is measured with the photometer for 20 minutes, with measure points each 20 seconds.
To analyze the volume activity of AraDH, dilutions are chosen, which have a linear increase of extinction for at least 180 seconds (ΔE).
The photometer software is used to calculate the extinction change per minute (ΔE/min). The negative control values are subtracted.

To calculate the AraDH volume activity, following formula is used: Volume activity [U/ml]= ΔE*min -1 *ε -1 *d -1 *D U: Enzyme activity (µmol*min -1 ) 1 U = 16.67 nkat ΔE/min: Change in extinction at 340 nm (min -1 ) ε: Extinction coefficient of NADPH at 340 nm (6.220 mM -1 *cm -1 ) d: thickness of the cuvette [cm] (300 μl in a 96 well MTP: 0.95 cm) D: Dilution of the sample

For quantification of protein concentration, the protein assay Pierce TM BCA Assay Kit is used. Suitable dilutions are measured according to the manufacturers instruction, adapted to the 96 well MTP. To generate a standard curve, that enables calculation of the sample protein concentration, bovine serum albumin (BSA) is used with concentrations from 0 mg/ml to 2 mg/ml. For the measurement, 20 µl of the sample dilutions as well as BSA dilutions are mixed with each 200 µl of pre mixed solution A+B (50:1). The solutions are incubated for at least 40 minutes at 37°C. The blue color, resulting from the biuret-reaction can be quantified photometrically at 562 nm.
The final step is the calculation of the specific enzyme activity in the SI unit [nkat/mg] that is done by dividing the volume activity [nkat/ml] with the protein concentration [mg/ml].
